comments.text: Aristotle accepted my lean for 460! Where should I send it? The following was proved by Aristotle: - theorem sum_prime_reciprocals_diverges : ¬Summable fun p : ℕ => if Nat.Prime p then (1 : ℝ) / p else 0 - theorem sum_prime_tail_diverges (N : ℕ) : ¬Summable fun p : ℕ => if Nat.Prime p ∧ N < p then (1 : ℝ) / p else 0 The following was negated by Aristotle: - theorem erdos_seq_exists (n : ℕ) : ∃ a : ℕ → ℕ, ErdosSeq n a Here is the code for the 'negate_state' tactic, used within these negations:

The easiest thing to do is go to Lean 4 Web (selecting Mathlib v4.24.0 on the top right, which I've already done in this link provided), copy-paste the Lean code there, then go to the location bar to copy the URL, and post a link here. Because the entire file is encoded in the "codez" parameter in the URL, it's best to use < a href = " link goes here " > ... < / a > in your forum comment so the link doesn't take up the whole page. Or you can post to GitHub or any other hosting solution.

This problem is stated differently in [Er77c] and [ErGr80]. An easier way to phrase the difference is that in [Er77c] we have $(a_k,n)=1$ for all $k\geq 1$ but this is not guaranteed in the [ErGr80] formulation. For example with $n=6$ the [Er77c] sequence begins $0,1,5$ while the [ErGr80] sequence begins $6,1,2$. As written, the sum trivially diverges, since it contains $a_k=n+p$ for all large primes $p$. This is the trivial solution posted on Reddit that natso26 mentions. Perhaps a condition like $a_k\leq n$ was intended, but neither source mentions this, and without any further reference or motivation given for this problem I don't think we can resolve what was actually meant. The proof posted by Przemek Chojecki would resolve this stronger version if $f(n)=\sum_{a<n} \frac{1}{a}1_{P^-(n-a)>a}\to \infty$. Averaging this over $n\leq N$ and using estimates on rough numbers show (I think) that it grows like $\log\log n$ on average. It's not clear to me whether this $\to \infty$ along all $n$ though.

You're right, in either formulation, if one sums over all $k$ without any upper cut-off on $a_k$, then the series is automatically divergent: for every prime $p>n$ the term $a=n+p$ must occur in the greedy sequence, because before reaching $n+p$ all previously chosen translates satisfy $n-a_i\in[-p+1,n-1]$, so none is divisible by $p$ (the only multiple of $p$ in that interval is $0$, which never arises as $n-a_i$ in these constructions); hence $\gcd(n-(n+p),\,n-a_i)=\gcd(p,\,n-a_i)=1$ for all earlier $i$, making $n+p$ admissible at the moment it becomes available and therefore impossible for the greedy rule to skip. Comparing $\sum_{p>n}1/(n+p)$ with $\sum_p 1/p$ gives divergence for every fixed $n\ge2$. If instead one imposes the natural ''nontrivial'' restriction $a_k<n$ (or $a_k\le n$), then the above argument disappears and one can at least prove an averaged divergence statement from the forced ''rough'' contribution. Writing $P^-(m)$ for the least prime factor of $|m|$ (with $P^-(\pm1)=\infty$), set\[ f(n):=\sum_{a=1}^{n-1}\frac1a\,\mathbf 1_{\{P^-(n-a)>a\}}, \]so that (by the forced-rough lemma) the truncated greedy sum $S_{<n}(n):=\sum_{a_k\le n-1}1/a_k$ satisfies $S_{<n}(n)\ge f(n)$ for every $n$ in both formulations. Averaging over $n\le N$ and changing variables $m=n-a$ yields $\sum_{n\le N}f(n)=\sum_{a\le N-1}\frac1a\,\Phi(N-a,a)$, where $\Phi(x,y)$ counts $y$-rough integers $\le x$; Buchstab’s asymptotic for $\Phi(x,y)$ (uniform for $a\in[N^{1/3},N^{1/2}]$, where $u=\log(N-a)/\log a\in[2,3]$ and hence $\omega(u)\gg1$) gives $\Phi(N-a,a)\gg N/\log a$ on that range, and thus\[ \frac1N\sum_{n\le N}f(n)\ \gg\ \sum_{N^{1/3}\le a\le N^{1/2}}\frac1{a\log a}\ \asymp\ \log\log N. \]In particular $\limsup_{n\to\infty}f(n)=\infty$ and hence $\limsup_{n\to\infty}S_{<n}(n)=\infty$; what remains open is whether $f(n)\to\infty$ (and thus $S_{<n}(n)\to\infty$) along all $n$. I've re-written the note to discuss both cases and give the full proofs. I think this solves the problem. See here: https://www.ulam.ai/research/erdos-460-v2.pdf
Your note still does not solve the problem, since it does not establish $f(n)\to \infty$ for all $n$, which is what is required.

You are right. Also I went deeper into trying to prove it, and it comes down to a conjecture which looks adjacent to recent results by Gafni-Tao, but is currently out of reach. I've updated https://www.ulam.ai/research/erdos-460-v2.pdf with the discussion around this dyadic strategy and a plausible conjecture that would imply this pointwise divergence. At least we have a clear picture what is interesting/hard about the problem.
What AI tools are you using?
GPT-5.2 mostly, but it's a more complicated discussion and exploring directions - I have a PhD in maths, on number theory but more algebraic flavour, so that definitely helps.

> I don't we can resolve what was actually meant. Perhaps it is best if, as a community, we agree to add, say, the condition $a_k\leq n$ to disallow trivial solutions? I don't think we can determine Erdos' original intent, but at least we can modify the problem so that it is non-trivial. I suppose another thing to decide is whether to directly edit this problem or to establish a new entry with the additional condition imposed. I'll leave Thomas to decide what to do here.
Agree with this. Ideally it can just be added in the comments below the current statement - as this is the statement found in Erdos' papers.

Yes, I think I'll add $a_k\leq n$ (with an explanation in the remarks). It doesn't seem interesting/significant enough to warrant creating another problem as a minor variant. I've tried without success to find anything hinting at this problem in the papers of Eggleton, Erdős, and Selfridge, but to no avail. The closest candidate might be this one from 1976 on lowest common multiples, but I can't see anything in there relevant to this problem, even in disguised form. Perhaps someone else will have more luck.

Thanks for looking through the literature and trying to establish the current status of the problem. As you have observed, the presence of people using AI has increased the workload in this regard. Thanks for your continued community guidance!
To add(?) to the confusion, I found a Reddit post claiming a solution here. I haven’t checked and don’t know if it’s correct, incorrect, or solves a wrong version as we seem to be discussing now.

I've looked through it and it's pretty wordy explanation that the sum trivially diverges, since it contains $a_k=n+p$ for all large primes $p$. It doesn't discuss two versions of the problem ($a_0$), nor talks about a harder version of $a_k < n$.
Thanks for doing the work of looking through claimed solutions, even though we already sort of know there's low chance of it succeeding!

As Kevin Barreto mentioned, we might have $a_0=0$ with $i=0$ included in $gcd$ condition. The full proof of both versions is here: https://www.ulam.ai/research/erdos-460.pdf - here's the sketch: Fix $n\ge2$ and define a greedy increasing sequence $(a_k)$ by requiring the translates $n-a_k$ to be pairwise coprime: in Case A one enforces $\gcd(n-a_k,n-a_i)=1$ for all $1\le i<k$, while in Case B one includes $i=0$ with $a_0=0$, which is equivalent to adding the standing constraint $\gcd(a_k,n)=1$. To study Erdős' divergence question for $\sum_k 1/a_k$ (and its natural subsums), the note introduces a dichotomy for $1\le a\le n-1$: call $a$ rough if the least prime factor $P^{-}(n-a)$ exceeds $a$ (equivalently, $n-a$ has no prime divisor $\le a$), and proper otherwise. This yields a canonical subsum $S_{\mathrm{rough}}(n)=\sum_{k:\,a_k\le n-1,\ P^{-}(n-a_k)>a_k}1/a_k$, with complementary ''proper'' subsum capturing the genuinely interactive part of the greedy dynamics. The main reduction is that the rough part is forced and admits an explicit description independent of the history: for any $a<n$ with $P^{-}(n-a)>a$, one has $\gcd(n-a,n-a')=1$ for every $a'<a$ (since any common prime divisor would divide $a-a'$ and hence be $\le a$), so such an $a$ is admissible at every stage once it becomes available (and in Case B this automatically includes coprimality with $n$). A greedy minimality argument then shows the process cannot skip any rough $a$: if $a$ were the least skipped rough integer, at the first step where $a_{k-1}<a<a_k$ it would be admissible, contradicting the definition of $a_k$ as the least admissible choice. Consequently the set of rough values appearing with $a_k<n$ is exactly $R(n)=\{1\le a\le n-1: P^{-}(n-a)>a\}$, and hence\[ S_{\mathrm{rough}}(n)=\sum_{a\in R(n)}\frac1a=\sum_{\substack{1\le a\le n-1\\ P^{-}(n-a)>a}}\frac1a, \]giving an explicit, history-free lower bound for $\sum_k 1/a_k$ in both cases and reducing the divergence problem to estimating this rough sum plus the remaining proper contribution.
> reducing the divergence problem to estimating this rough sum plus the remaining proper contribution If I'm understanding correctly, this means that the essence of the problem remains unresolved.
Yes, the URL in the comment essentially runs through the ideas laid out there but in a more formal way.

Indeed, this note v1 basically reduced the problem to its core of estimating a subsum. The full solution of the problem (divergence) with discussion is here in a re-written note v2: https://www.ulam.ai/research/erdos-460-v2.pdf This should settle the problem as is.
I was going through [Er77c], and on the bottom of page 22 of the PDF, I think we actually want $a_0=0$ and for all $0\leq i<k$, with the sum running from $i=1$.
I've asked GPT-5.2 Pro about this problem here. I agree with it that the problem is likely missing some condition like $a_k\leq n$. Can others have a look so we can establish the likely intent?

I'm not sure whether $a_0 = 0$ with $i=0$ included in $gcd$ condition is the right one, but both versions should be provable. See here: https://www.ulam.ai/research/erdos-460.pdf - I also write the summary of the proof below.
